event: A soul carried to heaven by angels
symbols: angels bearing the soul upward — divine messengers carrying the redeemed to paradise
young woman as the soul — the soul's purity and vulnerability in the passage from death to glory
golden clouds — the threshold of heaven, radiant with divine light
roses falling — the beauty and fragrance of sanctity, the reward of faithful life
dark and light wings — the contrasting aspects of angelic power and mercy
description: Two powerful winged angels carry a limp, unconscious young woman through golden-lit clouds. The angel at left has large dark wings and cradles the woman's upper body, while the angel at right has pale white-grey wings and supports her lower limbs. The woman wears a flowing pink-rose garment and her head falls back, eyes closed, hair loose. Small roses or flowers appear to fall from her hands. The sky behind them is suffused with warm golden and amber light breaking through heavy clouds. The figures are rendered with idealized academic anatomy and dramatic chiaroscuro.
theological_description: Bouguereau's Soul Carried to Heaven visualizes the Christian belief in the angelic transport of the righteous soul to paradise after death. This concept is rooted in Jesus's parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus (Luke 16:22): 'The time came when the beggar died and the angels carried him to Abraham's side.' The image also resonates with the broader biblical teaching on angels as ministering spirits sent to serve those who will inherit salvation (Hebrews 1:14). The soul's unconscious, surrendered posture — eyes closed, body limp — expresses complete trust and the passage through death into new life: 'To be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord' (2 Corinthians 5:8). The golden light toward which the angels ascend represents the divine glory that awaits the redeemed — the 'eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison' that Paul describes (2 Corinthians 4:17). The falling roses suggest the tradition of the saints' sweet fragrance (2 Corinthians 2:15), and the entire scene anticipates the promise of Revelation 21:4 that God will wipe away every tear and death shall be no more.

scripture_refs.reason: The angels carried Lazarus to Abraham's side — the direct scriptural basis for angelic transport of the soul
Angels as ministering spirits sent to serve those who will inherit salvation
To be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord — the soul's transition depicted
The eternal weight of glory — the golden light toward which the soul ascends
